title: Gauff Battles Through Stomach Virus to Win in Madrid
summary: Coco Gauff proved her toughness once again at the Madrid Open, fighting through a stomach virus to defeat Sorana Cirstea 4-6, 7-5, 6-1 and reach the fourth round. The American third seed was seen vomiting into a bin midway through the second set, but she refused to quit. "I don't know honestly" how she got through it, Gauff admitted afterward. "I think I got what everybody else is having here in Madrid, unfortunately. So, I'm just going to try to push through for tomorrow." Several players have been struck down by illness in Madrid this week. Six-time Grand Slam winner Iga Swiatek retired from her match on Saturday, saying she had "zero stability and zero energy." Marin Cilic withdrew before his second-round match, and Liudmila Samsonova also dropped out. Gauff asked for a medical timeout in the second set and was given medication. "I did start to feel better, not feeling like I had to throw up. They gave me some pills, so that definitely helped," she said. "But I was really tired... The first part was literally just trying to keep whatever I ate down. Once they gave me something to help with that, then I was just nauseous and tired. But I can deal with that." Gauff will face Czech 13th seed Linda Noskova in the last 16 as she continues her preparation for the French Open, where she is the reigning champion. Last year, she lost to Aryna Sabalenka in the Madrid final before avenging that defeat a month later at Roland Garros. This is Gauff's third straight year reaching the fourth round in Madrid.
why_it_matters: Gauff's resilience under physical distress shows that mental toughness is as important as skill in high-stakes sports. For young athletes, her example emphasizes that setbacks (like illness) don't have to end a performance—you can push through with help and determination.

keywords.explanation: An illness that affects the stomach and often causes vomiting or diarrhea.
The act of throwing up the contents of the stomach through the mouth.
A short break in a match when a player receives medical treatment.
Medicine given to treat an illness or relieve pain.
Feeling sick to the stomach, as if about to vomit.
The current title holder of a competition.

background_read: The Madrid Open is a clay-court tournament that often sees players struggle with illness due to the change in climate, pollen, and travel schedules. In 2026, a stomach virus spread through the player field, affecting several top competitors.

Coco Gauff (the 22-year-old American world No. 3) is the reigning French Open champion, having won her first Grand Slam title at Roland Garros in 2025. She is known for her fighting spirit and has often bounced back from tough situations, both on and off the court.

Iga Swiatek (a five-time Grand Slam winner) and Marin Cilic (former US Open champion) also had to withdraw from their matches due to illness, showing how widespread the problem was. The tournament medical staff provided medication to affected players, but some could not continue.

The fourth-round match against Linda Noskova (a Czech young star seeded 13th) will test Gauff's recovery. If she wins, she could set up a rematch of last year's final against Aryna Sabalenka, who has been dominant on hard courts but also strong on clay.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Gauff battles through stomach virus to win
└─ SPECIFIC: 'vomiting into a bin midway through second set'
├─ SET SCORES: lost first, won second after medical timeout, dominated third
└─ QUOTE: Gauff's uncertainty about how she got through it
├─ Swiatek retired ('zero stability and zero energy')
├─ Marin Cilic withdrew before second round
└─ Received medication (pills) that helped with nausea and vomiting
└─ History: lost to Sabalenka in 2025 Madrid final, then beat her at French Open

perspectives.description: Gauff's determination to continue despite vomiting and extreme fatigue is inspiring. She showed incredible mental strength and was rewarded with a come-from-behind win. This story will motivate fans and young players to never give up, even when their body is failing them.

While Gauff won, she pushed herself dangerously hard while ill. If her condition worsens, she could risk longer-term health issues. The spread of illness among players also raises questions about tournament hygiene, scheduling, and whether players should be allowed to postpone matches.

Illness outbreaks at tournaments are not uncommon, especially when players travel from different climates. The Madrid Open had several high-profile withdrawals, which changed the draw dramatically. Medical policies in tennis allow for on-court treatment, but there is no standard procedure for postponing matches due to contagious illness.

If Gauff recovers quickly, she could use this experience as a confidence boost for the French Open defense. Her next few matches will show whether the virus has lingering effects. The Madrid title is still within reach, and a strong run could set the stage for a successful clay season.
